Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.00 to 3.00 cycles (1.33x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is vectorized, but using 45% register length (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.00 to 3.40 cycles (1.18x speedup).

Workaround(s):
 - Read less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop


512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

It is main loop of related source loop which is unrolled by 8 (including vectorization).
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 64.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is vectorized, but using only 256 out of 512 bits (AVX/AVX2 instructions on AVX-512 processors).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 2.00 to 1.00 cycles (2.00x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

Workaround(s):

 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Section 6.3.1.1: Path #1
========================

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 9% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 25.00 to 1.56 cycles (16.00x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 25.00 to 23.00 cycles (1.09x speedup).

Workaround(s):
 - Read less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 5.00 to 1.00 cycles (5.00x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 8.00 to 1.50 cycles (5.33x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
8 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 8.00 to 1.00 cycles (8.00x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by execution of FP multiply or FMA (fused multiply-add) operations (the FP multiply/FMA unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 8.00 to 5.80 cycles (1.38x speedup).

Workaround(s):
Reduce the number of FP multiply/FMA instructions

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Section 6.15.1: Binary loop #3008
=================================

The related source loop is multi-versionned.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 64.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
8 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.00 to 1.12 cycles (8.00x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.00 to 7.40 cycles (1.22x speedup).

Workaround(s):
 - Read less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is vectorized, but using only 256 out of 512 bits (AVX/AVX2 instructions on AVX-512 processors).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Section 6.17.1: Binary loop #2997
=================================

The related source loop is not unrolled or unrolled with no peel/tail loop.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.29 out of 32.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 10.50 to 3.00 cycles (3.50x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
8 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 10.50 to 1.31 cycles (8.00x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Your loop is not vectorized.

By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.40 to 0.56 cycles (7.82x speedup).

 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by execution of FP multiply or FMA (fused multiply-add) operations (the FP multiply/FMA unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.50 to 3.00 cycles (1.50x speedup).

Workaround(s):
Reduce the number of FP multiply/FMA instructions

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.13x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck).

Workaround(s):
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop


512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Your loop is vectorized, but using only 256 out of 512 bits (AVX/AVX2 instructions on AVX-512 processors).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by execution of divide and square root operations (the divide/square root unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.00 to 3.00 cycles (1.33x speedup).

Workaround(s):
 - Reduce the number of division or square root instructions:
  * If denominator is constant over iterations, use reciprocal (replace x/y with x*(1/y)). Check precision impact. This will be done by your compiler with no-prec-div or Ofast
 - Check whether you really need double precision. If not, switch to single precision to speedup execution

Execution units bottlenecks

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.22x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).


Section 17.2: Source loop ending at line 190

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is probably not vectorized.
Only 15% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.00 to 0.50 cycles (6.00x speedup).
Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 3.00 to 2.60 cycles (1.15x speedup).

Workaround(s):
 - Read less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------

Workaround(s):
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop


512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.50 to 25.00 cycles (1.14x speedup).

 - Provide more information to your compiler:

Section 18.8.1.3: Path #3
=========================

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.50 to 14.00 cycles (2.11x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is vectorized, but using only 256 out of 512 bits (AVX/AVX2 instructions on AVX-512 processors).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck).

Workaround(s):
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop


512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.00 to 3.00 cycles (3.00x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
8 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.00 to 1.12 cycles (8.00x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.00 to 6.60 cycles (1.36x speedup).

Workaround(s):

 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop

Warnings:
 - Ignoring paths for analysis
 - Too many paths. Rerun with max-paths=9
 - RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths

Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.

This loop has 9 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
 - hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
 - turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)

Warnings:
Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
3% of peak computational performance is used (1.12 out of 32.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 28.60 to 17.00 cycles (1.68x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 16% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

17% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 16% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 6% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 14% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 11% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
 - 43% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Masked instructions
-------------------
Detected masked instructions.
Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your loop is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.
Workaround(s):
If your loop is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your loop. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.04x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 6.00 to 5.20 cycles (1.15x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

Section 23.2.1.1: Path #1
=========================

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
16 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.00 to 0.25 cycles (16.00x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is vectorized, but using only 256 out of 512 bits (AVX/AVX2 instructions on AVX-512 processors).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Masked instructions
-------------------
Detected masked instructions.
Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your loop is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.
Workaround(s):
If your loop is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your loop. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):

Vectorization

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by execution of FP multiply or FMA (fused multiply-add) operations (the FP multiply/FMA unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 5.00 to 4.20 cycles (1.19x speedup).

Workaround(s):
Reduce the number of FP multiply/FMA instructions

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.25x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 1.75 to 1.50 cycles (1.17x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by:
 - reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
 - writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)

Workaround(s):

 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop

 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.00x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Section 32.1.1.1: Path #1
=========================

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 10.00 to 4.00 cycles (2.50x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is vectorized, but using only 256 out of 512 bits (AVX/AVX2 instructions on AVX-512 processors).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).

Workaround(s):
Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by execution of FP multiply or FMA (fused multiply-add) operations (the FP multiply/FMA unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 10.00 to 6.60 cycles (1.52x speedup).

Workaround(s):
Reduce the number of FP multiply/FMA instructions

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Masked instructions
-------------------
Detected masked instructions.
Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your loop is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.
Workaround(s):
If your loop is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your loop. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 5.50 to 0.44 cycles (12.57x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

-------------
Your loop is not vectorized.
16 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 1.00 to 0.06 cycles (16.00x speedup).
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.

Workaround(s):
 - Try another compiler or update/tune your current one:
  * use the vec-report option to understand why your loop was not vectorized. If "existence of vector dependences", try the IVDEP directive. If, using IVDEP, "vectorization possible but seems inefficient", try the VECTOR ALWAYS directive.
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------

 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop


512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 34.40 to 24.50 cycles (1.40x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

Workaround(s):

 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

1% of peak computational performance is used (0.50 out of 32.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

Vectorization
-------------
Your loop is vectorized, but using only 256 out of 512 bits (AVX/AVX2 instructions on AVX-512 processors).

All SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers).


Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by execution of divide and square root operations (the divide/square root unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 8.00 to 1.40 cycles (5.71x speedup).

Workaround(s):
 - Reduce the number of division or square root instructions:
  * If denominator is constant over iterations, use reciprocal (replace x/y with x*(1/y)). Check precision impact. This will be done by your compiler with no-prec-div or Ofast
 - Check whether you really need double precision. If not, switch to single precision to speedup execution


512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Expensive FP math instructions/calls
------------------------------------
Detected performance impact from expensive FP math instructions/calls.
By removing/reexpressing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 8.00 to 1.40 cycles (5.71x speedup).

Masked instructions
-------------------
Detected masked instructions.
Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your loop is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.
Workaround(s):
If your loop is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your loop. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

Section 33.13.1: Binary loop #141331
====================================

The loop is defined in /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/motion/helium_interactions.F:811-816.

The related source loop is not unrolled or unrolled with no peel/tail loop.
This loop has 3 execution paths.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
 - hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
 - turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

Execution units bottlenecks
---------------------------
Performance is limited by writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck).

Workaround(s):
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop


512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 4.50 to 3.00 cycles (1.50x speedup).

FMA
---
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
Workaround(s):
 - Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use axHost or xHost
 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.

However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

The related source loop is not unrolled or unrolled with no peel/tail loop.
The structure of this loop is probably <if then [else] end>.

The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
 - hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
 - turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX


Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)

Section 36.1.1.1: Path #1
=========================

Code clean check
----------------
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 9.40 to 5.00 cycles (1.88x speedup).
Workaround(s):
 - Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop

 - Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.01x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).

512-bits vectorization
----------------------
On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

Workaround(s):
Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high
